corpusContext.failureShape: An informative image has alt text that is a filename, technical id, or generic placeholder ("image.jpg", "pim_bu_100093", "Slider Image 4", "icon", "cover"), so screen-reader users get a meaningless name instead of the image's content.
corpusContext.fix: Write alt text that conveys the image's meaning or purpose. Never use filenames, ids, or generic placeholders. If the image is purely decorative, use an empty alt (alt="") instead.

corpusContext.failureShape: A control that should work with the keyboard doesn't — a checkbox or radio won't toggle on Space, a button or link won't activate on Enter/Space — because a custom handler intercepts or replaces the native key behavior.
corpusContext.fix: Use native interactive elements (<button>, <a href>, <input>) and don't preventDefault their key handling. For custom controls, handle Enter and Space (and Arrow keys for radios) per the ARIA pattern so activation matches a click.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's text is generic and uninformative out of context ("click here", "more info", "read more", "details", "view more"), often repeated identically across the page, so its purpose and destination aren't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: A button (often icon-only or with only visual content) exposes no accessible name, so assistive tech announces it as an unlabeled "button".
corpusContext.fix: Give every button discernible text: visible text content, or an aria-label / aria-labelledby for icon-only buttons. Prefer a native <button> over a clickable <div>/<span>.

corpusContext.failureShape: An image's alt text is excessively long or redundant — a paragraph of text, or a concatenation that duplicates adjacent visible text — so screen-reader users hear bloated, repeated content.
corpusContext.fix: Keep alt text concise (typically under ~125 characters) and non-redundant. If the same information is already in adjacent text, mark the image decorative with alt="".

corpusContext.failureShape: An image has alt text that is present but wrong or insufficient — outdated, mismatched to the destination, or too vague to describe a complex visual — so the announced text misleads or under-informs screen-reader users.
corpusContext.fix: Make the alt text accurately and sufficiently describe the image's content and purpose in context. Update stale alt text and expand vague names so they match what the image actually conveys.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link opens in a new window/tab (target="_blank"), launches an external app (mailto:), or leaves for an external domain without warning the user in advance, so the context change is unexpected.
corpusContext.fix: Warn before the context change: include "(opens in a new window)" or "(external)" in the link's text or aria-label so the behavior is announced before activation.

corpusContext.fix: Use native interactive elements (<button>, <a href>, <input>) and don't preventDefault their key handling. For custom controls, handle Enter and Space (and Arrow keys for radios) per the ARIA pattern so activation matches a click.

corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link has no accessible name — it wraps only an icon, SVG, or image with empty alt, or is an empty <a> with no text — so screen-reader users hear only "link" with no idea of its purpose or destination.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link a discernible accessible name: visible text, an aria-label, or alt text on the link's image that names the destination. Remove empty/decorative links from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: Social media links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, etc.) carry only an icon glyph or SVG with no accessible name, so the platform they link to isn't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give each social link an aria-label naming the platform (e.g. aria-label="Instagram"), or add visually hidden text. Mark the icon itself aria-hidden="true".

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A "skip to main content" link exists but doesn't move focus when activated — focus stays on the link — so the bypass doesn't actually work.
corpusContext.fix: Point the skip link's href at the id of the main content container and give that target tabindex="-1" so the browser moves focus into it on activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page has no "skip to main content" link (or other bypass mechanism), so keyboard and screen-reader users must tab through the entire navigation/header on every page to reach the main content.
corpusContext.fix: Add a "Skip to main content" link as the first focusable element on the page, targeting the id of the main content region. Make it visible on focus.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link that opens a PDF, an external site, or a new window/tab doesn't signal that in its accessible name, so users can't anticipate the file type or context change before activating it.
corpusContext.fix: Include the target behavior in the link's accessible name: append "(PDF)", "(opens in a new window)", or "(external)" via the link text or an aria-label.

corpusContext.failureShape: A custom tab, toggle, slider indicator, or current navigation/language item does not expose its selected/pressed/current state programmatically, so assistive tech can't tell which item is active.
corpusContext.fix: Expose state on custom widgets: aria-selected="true" on the active tab, aria-pressed on toggles, or aria-current="page"/"true" on the current nav, language, or slide. Keep the attribute in sync as the active item changes.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form input (text field, combobox, date picker, textarea) has no programmatically associated label — it relies on a placeholder or nearby visual text — so assistive tech announces it without a name or with the wrong name.
corpusContext.fix: Associate every input with a real label: a <label for> matching the input id, or aria-label / aria-labelledby. Never rely on placeholder as the accessible name. For custom comboboxes, also link the visible label to the widget.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page's top-level heading structure is wrong: no H1, multiple H1s, the page starts at H2, or visually prominent headings are not marked up as headings at all — so screen-reader users get no reliable map of the page.
corpusContext.fix: Give each page exactly one H1 that names its main topic, mark every visual heading with a real heading tag, and reserve lower levels (H2–H6) for subsections. Don't use multiple H1s for sibling sections.

corpusContext.failureShape: Content that is visible on screen (article text, cards, list items, prices, navigation) is missing from the accessibility tree, so screen-reader users cannot reach or hear it — often because a wrapper carries aria-hidden="true", display is mismanaged on injected content, or dynamic content never renders into the tree.
corpusContext.fix: Make sure meaningful content is exposed to assistive tech: never put aria-hidden="true" on a container that holds real content, render route-changed/dynamically loaded content into the DOM and accessibility tree, and verify the main region is reachable in screen-reader browse mode. Manage focus to newly revealed content.

corpusContext.failureShape: Heading levels are non-sequential — the page jumps levels (e.g. H1 to H3, H2 to H6) or nests them out of order, so the heading outline misrepresents the document structure.
corpusContext.fix: Nest headings sequentially without skipping levels (H1 then H2 then H3…). Pick the level by the section's place in the outline, not by its visual size; use CSS for sizing.

corpusContext.failureShape: When a modal, dialog, panel, or menu overlay opens, keyboard focus stays on the trigger instead of moving into the new content, so screen-reader and keyboard users don't know it opened and tab through the background page first.
corpusContext.fix: On open, move focus to the dialog container (or its first focusable element / heading) and trap focus inside it. On close, return focus to the trigger.

corpusContext.failureShape: A button (often icon-only or with only visual content) exposes no accessible name, so assistive tech announces it as an unlabeled "button".
corpusContext.fix: Give every button discernible text: visible text content, or an aria-label / aria-labelledby for icon-only buttons. Prefer a native <button> over a clickable <div>/<span>.

corpusContext.failureShape: Decorative icons, icon-font glyphs, or raw unicode characters are announced literally (as character codes or symbol names), polluting a control's accessible name or the reading order.
corpusContext.fix: Mark decorative icons and separator glyphs aria-hidden="true". Replace raw unicode used as icons with a properly labeled icon element, and give any meaningful icon a text alternative.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form control (custom select, combobox, switch, radio, checkbox, slider, date picker, quantity stepper) has no programmatic name, or relies on its current value/placeholder, so assistive tech announces it without a label.
corpusContext.fix: Give every form control a programmatic name: a <label> associated by id (not placeholder-only), or aria-label / aria-labelledby. For custom controls, also expose the correct role and state.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A custom tab, toggle, slider indicator, or current navigation/language item does not expose its selected/pressed/current state programmatically, so assistive tech can't tell which item is active.
corpusContext.fix: Expose state on custom widgets: aria-selected="true" on the active tab, aria-pressed on toggles, or aria-current="page"/"true" on the current nav, language, or slide. Keep the attribute in sync as the active item changes.

corpusContext.component: clickable element with no interactive role
corpusContext.failureShape: An interactive control is built from a non-interactive element (a link, div, span, image, or heading acting as a button, tab, or toggle), so assistive tech announces the wrong role — or none — and keyboard users can't operate it.
corpusContext.fix: Use the right element for the behavior: a native <button> for actions, real tabs (role="tablist"/"tab"/"tabpanel"), or at minimum the correct role plus keyboard handling and tabindex. Prefer native HTML over re-roled divs/links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A visual list (navigation groups, benefits, cards, track lists) is built from generic elements instead of list markup, so screen-reader users don't hear it as a list or get an item count.
corpusContext.fix: Mark up lists with <ul>/<ol> and <li> so assistive tech announces the list and its length. Group each repeated item in its own <li>.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page lacks landmark regions (no <main>, <nav>, <header>, or <footer>), so screen-reader users can't jump to the primary content or navigate by region.
corpusContext.fix: Wrap major page sections in HTML5 landmarks: <main> for primary content, <nav> for navigation, <header>/<footer> (banner/contentinfo) for the page chrome. One main landmark per page.

corpusContext.failureShape: Tabular data (prices, rates, schedules, comparisons) is built from <div>s or plain text instead of a real table, or a table lacks header semantics, so screen-reader users can't tie a value to its row/column header.
corpusContext.fix: Mark up tabular data with a semantic <table>: <thead> with <th scope> header cells, <tbody> with <td> data cells, and a <caption> naming the table's purpose. Don't fake tables with divs.

corpusContext.failureShape: A modal, dialog, or overlay panel can't be dismissed from the keyboard — pressing Escape does nothing — forcing keyboard users to hunt for a close control or reload the page.
corpusContext.fix: Add an Escape key handler that closes the modal and returns focus to the trigger. Pair it with a visible, focusable close button.

corpusContext.failureShape: A container is marked aria-hidden="true" but still contains focusable/interactive descendants (often the page chrome behind an open modal), so keyboard and screen-reader users reach controls that are supposed to be hidden.
corpusContext.fix: Never put aria-hidden="true" on an element that contains focusable content. When a modal is open, mark sibling regions inert (which also removes them from the tab order) rather than aria-hidden alone, or remove the focusable children from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: An interactive control (search icon, language switcher, listing, menu toggle) is missing from the accessibility tree and tab order entirely, so it cannot be detected or operated by screen-reader or keyboard users — sometimes due to a server block or severe performance issues.
corpusContext.fix: Ensure interactive controls render as real <button>/<a> elements present in the DOM and accessibility tree, reachable in browse mode and the tab order. Don't gate critical UI behind aria-hidden, role="presentation", or scripts that never expose semantics.

corpusContext.failureShape: A trigger that shows/hides content (menu, dropdown, accordion, disclosure, "show more") does not expose its expanded/collapsed state or that it has a popup, so users don't know it's openable or whether it's open.
corpusContext.fix: Put aria-expanded="true|false" on the trigger and toggle it on open/close; add aria-haspopup for menus/dialogs. Associate the trigger with the controlled region via aria-controls. Use a native <button> as the trigger.

corpusContext.failureShape: A custom widget uses an invalid or contradictory role/ARIA: role="presentation" on an interactive iframe, multiple tabs marked aria-selected at once, a concatenated or wrong accessible name, a dialog with no name, or state attributes leaking between controls.
corpusContext.fix: Use roles and ARIA attributes only as the spec allows: pick the correct role, keep exactly one selected tab, name dialogs with aria-labelledby, and don't let one control inherit another's value. Prefer native HTML, which carries correct semantics for free.

corpusContext.failureShape: Multiple navigation landmarks lack unique accessible names (or a landmark is named after its first child), so screen-reader users can't distinguish primary navigation from secondary navigation when listing landmarks.
corpusContext.fix: Give each repeated navigation landmark a unique, descriptive aria-label or aria-labelledby (e.g. "Main navigation", "Language switcher") that reflects the whole region, not one child item.

corpusContext.failureShape: A control that should work with the keyboard doesn't — a checkbox or radio won't toggle on Space, a button or link won't activate on Enter/Space — because a custom handler intercepts or replaces the native key behavior.
corpusContext.fix: Use native interactive elements (<button>, <a href>, <input>) and don't preventDefault their key handling. For custom controls, handle Enter and Space (and Arrow keys for radios) per the ARIA pattern so activation matches a click.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's text is generic and uninformative out of context ("click here", "more info", "read more", "details", "view more"), often repeated identically across the page, so its purpose and destination aren't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link has no accessible name — it wraps only an icon, SVG, or image with empty alt, or is an empty <a> with no text — so screen-reader users hear only "link" with no idea of its purpose or destination.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link a discernible accessible name: visible text, an aria-label, or alt text on the link's image that names the destination. Remove empty/decorative links from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: Social media links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, etc.) carry only an icon glyph or SVG with no accessible name, so the platform they link to isn't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give each social link an aria-label naming the platform (e.g. aria-label="Instagram"), or add visually hidden text. Mark the icon itself aria-hidden="true".

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A "skip to main content" link exists but doesn't move focus when activated — focus stays on the link — so the bypass doesn't actually work.
corpusContext.fix: Point the skip link's href at the id of the main content container and give that target tabindex="-1" so the browser moves focus into it on activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page has no "skip to main content" link (or other bypass mechanism), so keyboard and screen-reader users must tab through the entire navigation/header on every page to reach the main content.
corpusContext.fix: Add a "Skip to main content" link as the first focusable element on the page, targeting the id of the main content region. Make it visible on focus.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link that opens a PDF, an external site, or a new window/tab doesn't signal that in its accessible name, so users can't anticipate the file type or context change before activating it.
corpusContext.fix: Include the target behavior in the link's accessible name: append "(PDF)", "(opens in a new window)", or "(external)" via the link text or an aria-label.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link opens in a new window/tab (target="_blank"), launches an external app (mailto:), or leaves for an external domain without warning the user in advance, so the context change is unexpected.
corpusContext.fix: Warn before the context change: include "(opens in a new window)" or "(external)" in the link's text or aria-label so the behavior is announced before activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link opens in a new window/tab (target="_blank"), launches an external app (mailto:), or leaves for an external domain without warning the user in advance, so the context change is unexpected.
corpusContext.fix: Warn before the context change: include "(opens in a new window)" or "(external)" in the link's text or aria-label so the behavior is announced before activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: A control that should work with the keyboard doesn't — a checkbox or radio won't toggle on Space, a button or link won't activate on Enter/Space — because a custom handler intercepts or replaces the native key behavior.
corpusContext.fix: Use native interactive elements (<button>, <a href>, <input>) and don't preventDefault their key handling. For custom controls, handle Enter and Space (and Arrow keys for radios) per the ARIA pattern so activation matches a click.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's text is generic and uninformative out of context ("click here", "more info", "read more", "details", "view more"), often repeated identically across the page, so its purpose and destination aren't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link has no accessible name — it wraps only an icon, SVG, or image with empty alt, or is an empty <a> with no text — so screen-reader users hear only "link" with no idea of its purpose or destination.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link a discernible accessible name: visible text, an aria-label, or alt text on the link's image that names the destination. Remove empty/decorative links from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: Social media links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, etc.) carry only an icon glyph or SVG with no accessible name, so the platform they link to isn't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give each social link an aria-label naming the platform (e.g. aria-label="Instagram"), or add visually hidden text. Mark the icon itself aria-hidden="true".

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A "skip to main content" link exists but doesn't move focus when activated — focus stays on the link — so the bypass doesn't actually work.
corpusContext.fix: Point the skip link's href at the id of the main content container and give that target tabindex="-1" so the browser moves focus into it on activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page has no "skip to main content" link (or other bypass mechanism), so keyboard and screen-reader users must tab through the entire navigation/header on every page to reach the main content.
corpusContext.fix: Add a "Skip to main content" link as the first focusable element on the page, targeting the id of the main content region. Make it visible on focus.

corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link has no accessible name — it wraps only an icon, SVG, or image with empty alt, or is an empty <a> with no text — so screen-reader users hear only "link" with no idea of its purpose or destination.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link a discernible accessible name: visible text, an aria-label, or alt text on the link's image that names the destination. Remove empty/decorative links from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: Social media links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, etc.) carry only an icon glyph or SVG with no accessible name, so the platform they link to isn't conveyed.

corpusContext.failureShape: A "skip to main content" link exists but doesn't move focus when activated — focus stays on the link — so the bypass doesn't actually work.

corpusContext.fix: Implement the WAI-ARIA keyboard pattern for the widget (Enter/Space to open, Arrow keys to move, aria-activedescendant or roving tabindex for the active option), or replace it with a native <select>/<input> that gets keyboard support for free.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form input (text field, combobox, date picker, textarea) has no programmatically associated label — it relies on a placeholder or nearby visual text — so assistive tech announces it without a name or with the wrong name.
corpusContext.fix: Associate every input with a real label: a <label for> matching the input id, or aria-label / aria-labelledby. Never rely on placeholder as the accessible name. For custom comboboxes, also link the visible label to the widget.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page's top-level heading structure is wrong: no H1, multiple H1s, the page starts at H2, or visually prominent headings are not marked up as headings at all — so screen-reader users get no reliable map of the page.
corpusContext.fix: Give each page exactly one H1 that names its main topic, mark every visual heading with a real heading tag, and reserve lower levels (H2–H6) for subsections. Don't use multiple H1s for sibling sections.

corpusContext.failureShape: Content that is visible on screen (article text, cards, list items, prices, navigation) is missing from the accessibility tree, so screen-reader users cannot reach or hear it — often because a wrapper carries aria-hidden="true", display is mismanaged on injected content, or dynamic content never renders into the tree.
corpusContext.fix: Make sure meaningful content is exposed to assistive tech: never put aria-hidden="true" on a container that holds real content, render route-changed/dynamically loaded content into the DOM and accessibility tree, and verify the main region is reachable in screen-reader browse mode. Manage focus to newly revealed content.

corpusContext.failureShape: Heading levels are non-sequential — the page jumps levels (e.g. H1 to H3, H2 to H6) or nests them out of order, so the heading outline misrepresents the document structure.
corpusContext.fix: Nest headings sequentially without skipping levels (H1 then H2 then H3…). Pick the level by the section's place in the outline, not by its visual size; use CSS for sizing.

corpusContext.failureShape: Decorative icons, icon-font glyphs, or raw unicode characters are announced literally (as character codes or symbol names), polluting a control's accessible name or the reading order.
corpusContext.fix: Mark decorative icons and separator glyphs aria-hidden="true". Replace raw unicode used as icons with a properly labeled icon element, and give any meaningful icon a text alternative.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form control (custom select, combobox, switch, radio, checkbox, slider, date picker, quantity stepper) has no programmatic name, or relies on its current value/placeholder, so assistive tech announces it without a label.
corpusContext.fix: Give every form control a programmatic name: a <label> associated by id (not placeholder-only), or aria-label / aria-labelledby. For custom controls, also expose the correct role and state.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A custom tab, toggle, slider indicator, or current navigation/language item does not expose its selected/pressed/current state programmatically, so assistive tech can't tell which item is active.
corpusContext.fix: Expose state on custom widgets: aria-selected="true" on the active tab, aria-pressed on toggles, or aria-current="page"/"true" on the current nav, language, or slide. Keep the attribute in sync as the active item changes.

corpusContext.component: clickable element with no interactive role
corpusContext.failureShape: An interactive control is built from a non-interactive element (a link, div, span, image, or heading acting as a button, tab, or toggle), so assistive tech announces the wrong role — or none — and keyboard users can't operate it.
corpusContext.fix: Use the right element for the behavior: a native <button> for actions, real tabs (role="tablist"/"tab"/"tabpanel"), or at minimum the correct role plus keyboard handling and tabindex. Prefer native HTML over re-roled divs/links.

corpusContext.failureShape: A visual list (navigation groups, benefits, cards, track lists) is built from generic elements instead of list markup, so screen-reader users don't hear it as a list or get an item count.
corpusContext.fix: Mark up lists with <ul>/<ol> and <li> so assistive tech announces the list and its length. Group each repeated item in its own <li>.

corpusContext.failureShape: The page lacks landmark regions (no <main>, <nav>, <header>, or <footer>), so screen-reader users can't jump to the primary content or navigate by region.
corpusContext.fix: Wrap major page sections in HTML5 landmarks: <main> for primary content, <nav> for navigation, <header>/<footer> (banner/contentinfo) for the page chrome. One main landmark per page.

corpusContext.failureShape: Tabular data (prices, rates, schedules, comparisons) is built from <div>s or plain text instead of a real table, or a table lacks header semantics, so screen-reader users can't tie a value to its row/column header.
corpusContext.fix: Mark up tabular data with a semantic <table>: <thead> with <th scope> header cells, <tbody> with <td> data cells, and a <caption> naming the table's purpose. Don't fake tables with divs.

corpusContext.failureShape: A container is marked aria-hidden="true" but still contains focusable/interactive descendants (often the page chrome behind an open modal), so keyboard and screen-reader users reach controls that are supposed to be hidden.
corpusContext.fix: Never put aria-hidden="true" on an element that contains focusable content. When a modal is open, mark sibling regions inert (which also removes them from the tab order) rather than aria-hidden alone, or remove the focusable children from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: An interactive control (search icon, language switcher, listing, menu toggle) is missing from the accessibility tree and tab order entirely, so it cannot be detected or operated by screen-reader or keyboard users — sometimes due to a server block or severe performance issues.
corpusContext.fix: Ensure interactive controls render as real <button>/<a> elements present in the DOM and accessibility tree, reachable in browse mode and the tab order. Don't gate critical UI behind aria-hidden, role="presentation", or scripts that never expose semantics.

corpusContext.failureShape: A trigger that shows/hides content (menu, dropdown, accordion, disclosure, "show more") does not expose its expanded/collapsed state or that it has a popup, so users don't know it's openable or whether it's open.
corpusContext.fix: Put aria-expanded="true|false" on the trigger and toggle it on open/close; add aria-haspopup for menus/dialogs. Associate the trigger with the controlled region via aria-controls. Use a native <button> as the trigger.

corpusContext.failureShape: A custom widget uses an invalid or contradictory role/ARIA: role="presentation" on an interactive iframe, multiple tabs marked aria-selected at once, a concatenated or wrong accessible name, a dialog with no name, or state attributes leaking between controls.
corpusContext.fix: Use roles and ARIA attributes only as the spec allows: pick the correct role, keep exactly one selected tab, name dialogs with aria-labelledby, and don't let one control inherit another's value. Prefer native HTML, which carries correct semantics for free.

corpusContext.failureShape: Multiple navigation landmarks lack unique accessible names (or a landmark is named after its first child), so screen-reader users can't distinguish primary navigation from secondary navigation when listing landmarks.
corpusContext.fix: Give each repeated navigation landmark a unique, descriptive aria-label or aria-labelledby (e.g. "Main navigation", "Language switcher") that reflects the whole region, not one child item.

corpusContext.failureShape: A dynamic status, loading state, autocomplete suggestions, validation error, or value change is not exposed to assistive tech (no live region / stale aria-invalid / no value text), so screen-reader users miss the update.
corpusContext.fix: Announce dynamic changes with a live region (aria-live="polite", or role="status"/"alert"); keep aria-invalid and error messages in sync; use the combobox pattern (aria-expanded/aria-controls/aria-activedescendant) for autocomplete. corpusContext.failureShape: A form input (text field, combobox, date picker, textarea) has no programmatically associated label — it relies on a placeholder or nearby visual text — so assistive tech announces it without a name or with the wrong name.
corpusContext.fix: Associate every input with a real label: a <label for> matching the input id, or aria-label / aria-labelledby. Never rely on placeholder as the accessible name. For custom comboboxes, also link the visible label to the widget.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form input (search box, text field, combobox) has no visible, programmatically associated label and relies on a placeholder for its name, so the label disappears on input and is unreliable for assistive tech.
corpusContext.fix: Give every input a persistent visible <label> associated by id, or an aria-label when a visible label isn't possible. Never use placeholder text as the only label.

corpusContext.failureShape: A form control (custom select, combobox, switch, radio, checkbox, slider, date picker, quantity stepper) has no programmatic name, or relies on its current value/placeholder, so assistive tech announces it without a label.
corpusContext.fix: Give every form control a programmatic name: a <label> associated by id (not placeholder-only), or aria-label / aria-labelledby. For custom controls, also expose the correct role and state.

corpusContext.failureShape: A validation error is shown visually but is not programmatically tied to its field — the input lacks aria-invalid and aria-describedby — and often isn't announced, so screen-reader users learn an entry is wrong but not which field or why.
corpusContext.fix: Mark the failing input aria-invalid="true" and point aria-describedby at the error message element, and surface the message in a live region. Identify the field and the reason in text.

corpusContext.failureShape: An informative image has alt text that is a filename, technical id, or generic placeholder ("image.jpg", "pim_bu_100093", "Slider Image 4", "icon", "cover"), so screen-reader users get a meaningless name instead of the image's content.
corpusContext.fix: Write alt text that conveys the image's meaning or purpose. Never use filenames, ids, or generic placeholders. If the image is purely decorative, use an empty alt (alt="") instead.

corpusContext.failureShape: An image's alt text is excessively long or redundant — a paragraph of text, or a concatenation that duplicates adjacent visible text — so screen-reader users hear bloated, repeated content.
corpusContext.fix: Keep alt text concise (typically under ~125 characters) and non-redundant. If the same information is already in adjacent text, mark the image decorative with alt="".

corpusContext.failureShape: An image has alt text that is present but wrong or insufficient — outdated, mismatched to the destination, or too vague to describe a complex visual — so the announced text misleads or under-informs screen-reader users.
corpusContext.fix: Make the alt text accurately and sufficiently describe the image's content and purpose in context. Update stale alt text and expand vague names so they match what the image actually conveys.

corpusContext.failureShape: A complex image (chart, diagram, SVG/div-based graphic, or gallery) has no text alternative or position information, so the data or sequence it conveys is lost to screen-reader users.
corpusContext.fix: Give complex images a text alternative that conveys their data or purpose (e.g. a description or adjacent data table for charts). For galleries, expose position with aria-setsize/aria-posinset or descriptive alt per item.

corpusContext.failureShape: Decorative icons — icon-font glyphs, chevrons, or raw unicode characters — are announced by the screen reader as character codes or symbols, adding noise with no information.
corpusContext.fix: Hide decorative icons from assistive tech with aria-hidden="true". For icons that carry meaning, provide a text alternative (aria-label or an SVG <title>) instead.

corpusContext.failureShape: An image has no alt attribute at all (or is a background image standing in for content), often inside an image-only link, leaving the element with an empty accessible name.
corpusContext.fix: Add an alt attribute to every : descriptive text for informative images, or alt="" for decorative ones. For image-only links, the alt text must name the link destination.

corpusContext.fix: Add a "Skip to main content" link as the first focusable element on the page, targeting the id of the main content region. Make it visible on focus.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link that opens a PDF, an external site, or a new window/tab doesn't signal that in its accessible name, so users can't anticipate the file type or context change before activating it.
corpusContext.fix: Include the target behavior in the link's accessible name: append "(PDF)", "(opens in a new window)", or "(external)" via the link text or an aria-label.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link opens in a new window/tab (target="_blank"), launches an external app (mailto:), or leaves for an external domain without warning the user in advance, so the context change is unexpected.
corpusContext.fix: Warn before the context change: include "(opens in a new window)" or "(external)" in the link's text or aria-label so the behavior is announced before activation.

corpusContext.failureShape: A control that should work with the keyboard doesn't — a checkbox or radio won't toggle on Space, a button or link won't activate on Enter/Space — because a custom handler intercepts or replaces the native key behavior.
corpusContext.fix: Use native interactive elements (<button>, <a href>, <input>) and don't preventDefault their key handling. For custom controls, handle Enter and Space (and Arrow keys for radios) per the ARIA pattern so activation matches a click.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's text is generic and uninformative out of context ("click here", "more info", "read more", "details", "view more"), often repeated identically across the page, so its purpose and destination aren't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Write link text that describes the destination on its own, or add a unique aria-label / visually hidden suffix so repeated links are distinguishable (e.g. "Read more about admissions").

corpusContext.failureShape: A link has no accessible name — it wraps only an icon, SVG, or image with empty alt, or is an empty <a> with no text — so screen-reader users hear only "link" with no idea of its purpose or destination.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link a discernible accessible name: visible text, an aria-label, or alt text on the link's image that names the destination. Remove empty/decorative links from the tab order.

corpusContext.failureShape: A link's accessible name is noise or wrong — a raw URL, a build/slug prefix, a stray symbol, an over-long auto-generated description, or alt text that contradicts the destination — so the announced name doesn't match the link's purpose.
corpusContext.fix: Set a clean, accurate accessible name that matches the destination. Strip URLs, technical prefixes, and stray glyphs from the name; fix image alt text inside links so it describes where the link goes.

corpusContext.failureShape: Social media links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, etc.) carry only an icon glyph or SVG with no accessible name, so the platform they link to isn't conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give each social link an aria-label naming the platform (e.g. aria-label="Instagram"), or add visually hidden text. Mark the icon itself aria-hidden="true".

corpusContext.failureShape: A link exposes no accessible name (icon-only, logo, image-only, or empty), so its purpose and destination are not conveyed.
corpusContext.fix: Give every link discernible text or an aria-label that names the destination. For image links, the image needs alt text that describes where the link goes. Remove empty placeholder links.
